content.text: Why Dependency Analysis Matters
Most real-world tasks are not independent.
Dependency analysis

maps which tasks must finish before others can start. Without it, a pipeline either serializes everything (wasting parallel capacity) or runs tasks before their inputs exist (producing errors or hallucinations). A well-analyzed dependency graph guides three things: execution order, parallelism, and context passing.

attrs.alt: Task dependency graph showing T1-T7 with directional edges
attrs.title: Dependency graph: entry nodes (T1) fan out to parallel work (T4, T5), converge at integration (T6), then deploy (T7)

content.text: Rule of thumb: if task B uses any output from task A, B depends on A. Everything else can run in parallel.
Representing Dependencies
The minimal data structure attaches a
depends_on
list to each task. The graph is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) — acyclic because a cycle would make execution impossible:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class Task:
    id: str
    description: str
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    result: Optional[str] = None
    status: str = "pending"  # pending | ready | running | done | failed

TASKS = [
    Task("T1", "Parse project requirements"),
    Task("T2", "Design database schema", depends_on=["T1"]),
    Task("T3", "Scaffold API structure",  depends_on=["T1"]),
    Task("T4", "Write unit tests",        depends_on=["T2"]),
    Task("T5", "Implement endpoints",     depends_on=["T2", "T3"]),
    Task("T6", "Integration test",        depends_on=["T4", "T5"]),
    Task("T7", "Deploy to staging",       depends_on=["T6"]),
]
Extracting Dependencies with an LLM

When the task list is generated by the LLM (not hand-coded), dependencies must also be extracted automatically. A structured prompt asks the model to reason about data flow rather than execution order:

EXTRACT_DEPS_PROMPT = """
You are given a list of tasks for a project.
For each task, identify which other tasks (by ID) MUST complete before it can start.
Only list direct dependencies — skip transitive ones.
A dependency exists when one task's output is a required input to another.

Tasks:
{tasks}

Return JSON:
{{"dependencies": {{"<task_id>": ["<dep_id>", ...], ...}}}}
Tasks with no prerequisites get an empty list.
"""

def extract_dependencies(tasks: list[Task]) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    task_list = "\n".join(f"- {t.id}: {t.description}" for t in tasks)
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            EXTRACT_DEPS_PROMPT.format(tasks=task_list)}],
    )
    data = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return data["dependencies"]  # {"T1": [], "T2": ["T1"], ...}
What to instruct the LLM:
ask for direct dependencies only, not transitive ones. 'T5 needs T2' is a direct dependency; 'T5 also needs T1 transitively through T2' is not — Kahn's algorithm handles transitivity automatically.
Topological Wave Scheduling
Kahn's algorithm (Breadth-First topological sort) converts the DAG into
execution waves
. All tasks in wave N can run concurrently, and all wave-N tasks complete before wave N+1 starts:

The wave structure exposes parallelism naturally: T2 and T3 run concurrently in wave 1, and T4 and T5 run concurrently in wave 2 — even though T5 depends on both T2 and T3 while T4 depends only on T2.
Cycle Detection and Repair
A cycle in the dependency graph (T2 → T3 → T4 → T2) makes the topological sort hang. Depth-first search with three-color marking detects cycles early; an LLM can then propose which edge to remove:

Cycles usually arise when the LLM over-constrains dependencies — marking a task as dependent on another when the data flow is actually bidirectional or merely incidental. The repair prompt asks the model to reconsider whether the dependency is truly necessary.
Critical Path Analysis
The
critical path

is the longest dependency chain from start to finish. It determines the minimum total execution time, regardless of how much parallelism is available. Identifying it early lets you prioritize slow tasks and expose where a delay will ripple through the entire plan:

CRITICAL_PATH_PROMPT = """
Given these tasks and their dependencies, identify the critical path:
the sequence of tasks where any delay directly delays the entire project.

Tasks (id, estimated_hours, depends_on):
{task_table}

Return JSON:
{{"critical_path": ["T1", "T2", ...], "total_hours": <n>,
  "parallel_savings": <hours_saved_vs_sequential>}}
"""

def find_critical_path(tasks: list[Task], hours: dict[str, float]) -> dict:
    rows = "\n".join(
        f"  {t.id}: {hours.get(t.id, 1)}h, needs [{', '.join(t.depends_on)}]"
        for t in tasks
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            CRITICAL_PATH_PROMPT.format(task_table=rows)}],
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)

# Example:
# hours = {"T1": 1, "T2": 2, "T3": 1, "T4": 2, "T5": 3, "T6": 2, "T7": 1}
# result = find_critical_path(TASKS, hours)
# -> critical_path: T1 -> T2 -> T5 -> T6 -> T7 (9h)
# -> parallel_savings: 4h  (T3+T4 overlap T2+T5)
Executing the DAG
With waves computed, execution becomes a loop: run one wave (all tasks in parallel), collect results, pass those results as context to the next wave:

async def run_wave(wave_tasks: list[Task], index: dict[str, Task]) -> None:
    """Execute one wave of tasks concurrently.

    Each task in the wave receives the results of its dependencies
    as context, then runs via the LLM.
    """
    async def run_one(task: Task) -> None:
        dep_context = "\n".join(
            f"Result of {dep}: {index[dep].result}"
            for dep in task.depends_on
        )
        prompt = (
            f"Complete the following task: {task.description}\n\n"
            f"{('Context from prerequisites:\n' + dep_context) if dep_context else ''}"
        )
        task.status = "running"
        response = await async_client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=2048,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        task.result = response.content[0].text
        task.status = "done"

    await asyncio.gather(*(run_one(t) for t in wave_tasks))

async def execute_dag(tasks: list[Task]) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Run all tasks respecting dependency order."""
    index = {t.id: t for t in tasks}
    waves = topological_waves(tasks)
    for i, wave in enumerate(waves):
        print(f"Wave {i}: running {wave} in parallel")
        await run_wave([index[tid] for tid in wave], index)
    return {t.id: t.result for t in tasks}
The key detail is
context passing

: each task receives the results of its dependencies in the prompt. This is the mechanism that makes DAG execution more than just scheduling — it ensures each task has the information it needs to do its work correctly.
Dependency Pitfalls
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
Over-constraining
Wave size 1 everywhere; no parallelism
Re-examine whether data actually flows between tasks
Under-constraining
Task runs before its input exists
Audit each task's prompt for references to earlier outputs
Missing cycle check
Topological sort hangs at runtime
Detect cycles right after LLM returns the dependency map
Stale context
Task uses an older version of a dependency result
Always pass the final result, not intermediate ones
Bloated dependency context
Token budget exhausted on large DAGs
Summarize long results before passing as context
When to Re-Analyze Dependencies
After task failure:
a failed task may need to be replaced with a different approach, which changes its inputs and therefore its successors' dependencies.
After dynamic expansion:
if execution reveals that a task needs to be split into sub-tasks, run dependency analysis on the new sub-task set before scheduling.
After scope change:
when the user adds or removes requirements mid-execution, the dependency graph may need to be regenerated from scratch.
When the critical path shifts:
if a previously fast task is taking longer than estimated, re-check whether tasks can be reordered or parallelized further.
Summary
Dependency analysis
converts a flat task list into a DAG, enabling correct ordering and maximum parallelism.
LLM-extracted dependencies
require a structured prompt focused on data flow ('which outputs does this task consume?'), not execution order.
Topological wave scheduling
groups tasks into concurrent batches; Kahn's algorithm is the standard implementation.
Cycle detection
(DFS with 3-color marking) must run before topological sort — an undetected cycle causes an infinite loop.
Critical path analysis
identifies the longest chain and determines the minimum feasible completion time.
Context passing
at wave boundaries ensures each task has the results of its prerequisites injected into its prompt.
